speaker: interviewer
text: OK, take-- this is tape two, April 2, 1995. Survivor is Gregory Leyfell. The interviewer as Pamela Goodman. We're in Jamaica, New York. The country is the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I go back for a minute?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a-- I just remembered one interesting fact. While being in Lvov, in Lemberg, the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what year this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 1941-- yeah, right away, at the beginning, again. And the German sergeant, or whatever he was-- we were carrying benches. It was a school. And they were occupying the school. And they took some-- I don't know, about 10 or 15 us Jewish guys to carry the benches. And then from-- while I was carrying the benches, of one of the German-- of German soldiers, whatever it is, came over and looked around and whispered into my ear and told me, in German, that Hitler is not going to win this war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, I was afraid this is a provocation. So I ignored it and didn't react, didn't say a word. But he took-- took me and asked me to follow him. And he took me into one of the rooms, and closed the room, and disappeared for a-- went away. Didn't say a word. And after five minutes, he came back with a-- with food, and gave me the food, you know. And this was such an unusual event that I don't want to-- not to tell you about that. Because this happened at the beginning, all right? Now we are on the mountains, right? We are on the mountains. And I remembered that this [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna, ?] this Russian woman, who, to put it-- I don't want to say it she fell in love with me. But she-- she loved me more than any of the mothers I knew around, you know. So I-- I count on her-- counted on her. And I told him, I have to go to her. This is your-- the person that you ran away with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. The one who-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I said-- but I was not a native. I wasn't-- I-- see, I didn't know the city. And I couldn't go through the streets. Because the blood was still running. Then everybody would know right away that I ran away from the ghetto. Then we had to go somehow to a cemetery, to reach her house. But then I have to ask him to help me, to-- to show me how to go there. Because I couldn't-- I couldn't find the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: This man came from Krzemieniec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was a native son, and native son. This is the one who left his wife and the two daughters. So we went together. I had to take him. And it was late in the evening. We came to her house and knocked the window. And she was a saint, a real saint. She took in not only myself, but she took him, too, you know. And she jeopardized her life, you know? And so she took us, both of us. And she had a barn. It was a small town. You know, they had the house. But that-- I don't know, over the-- a little-- on the same territories, they had a barn. And the barn had a-- under the roof a, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: A loft or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So she-- in there she had some hay, you know, food for the cows or something. And then she-- we went there. We went there. So she had to feed us. And they were poor themselves. They were poor themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there people living with her, as well? Were there other people living with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she and her husband. Her husband, by the way, was Polish. But somehow, he behaved nicely. And he accepted it. But they were poor. But she has such a special feeling for me, you know? But how-- what shall we do with other guy, you know-- with the other guy? And he had some relatives in a town about 40 miles from Krzemieniec, from this city. So she managed to find a guy how-- who lived somehow on the same direction. And he took him. He went with him. And he hoped to reach the other town, where he had relatives. So he-- then I was the only one there. But [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] had to bring me food-- twice a day, whatever. And the neighbors, from the windows, I suppose, they saw something suspicious that she-- that repeats itself every day-- you know, every day. Then I suppose they denounced her to the Germans. And one day, I was lying on this-- on the attic, you know, in the hay. I hear the German language. And I look through the-- you know, the-- [? haystack. ?] And I see two German gendarmes. So I didn't know what to do with myself. I was in the hay. So I was sleeping. So I somehow tried to-- to mix up a little bit the hay, you know, so it won't [INAUDIBLE]. But underneath there, was a woman who brought some-- some grain. They were not supposed to do it-- against the law. So she came here, from the village, with the-- with the, you know, the grain. And she was on the main floor. And I was on the attic. And when the Germans started to walk up a ladder to the attic, I jumped down. She didn't hear me. She didn't see me. She was there. She didn't hear. Now, we come into a situation that is-- you can-- logic fails. You can only say it was a miracle, if somebody is religious. You can say whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, she didn't see me. And she didn't hear me. And these two German gendarmes-- they couldn't-- they didn't find me. They didn't find anything. And then they had to make a report. And then I hear one says to the other, in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means, in English, take a look. How much grain is in the sack? They had to put it in the report. So he comes in, into the barn, with a flashlight. And I was lying on the floor, next to the grain. And I hear him. He says, [GERMAN]. And somehow, he didn't see me. He didn't-- He. Didn't see me. What was going through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when the gendarmes left, I went. And [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] kissed me, and said-- and said, Grisha, you are under the protection of the Holy Spirit. This-- this was it. But this place was already compromised, shall we say, correct, you know? So I couldn't continue to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the other man who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The other man was taken-- he went-- somebody took him to go to another town, [NON-ENGLISH]. The name of it was [NON-ENGLISH]. So I was the only one. But I can tell is that Maria [? Nikichna ?] had one chicken. And since I looked so bad, she killed the chicken for me, to feed me. So I was [INAUDIBLE] what for to do now, what to do. Then I said-- the next night, I slept. There were some strawberries, or other, you know, in the-- in the outdoors. And it was November. It was already cold, it was. And I [? sat through ?] there. And we still didn't know what to do with me. She didn't know what to do with me. She couldn't tell me, go. Because if I go, they-- the Germans will kill me right way. So we-- we knew. Again, comes in here [INAUDIBLE]. My brother had this grocery store. So one another customer of there, a Russian family-- a Russian family-- and Maria [? Nikichna ?] knew them, too. We knew them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went to them. And this was a strange family. It were two sisters. There's no father, no mother, two sisters, rather spinsters-like, you know, and one man. And one man, the man, who was also from the Russian aristocracy-- and he was a radio engineer. At that time, the-- nobody could have a radio, you know, only the Germans. So they-- he used to repair. The Germans went to him. He used to repair it the Germans. So [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] went there. And she convinced them to take me in, to take me in. They-- they were fantastic people. Very-- the ladies were very religious, very religious. And they believed that if something will happen to them, it will happen with me there or without me. They just believed, you know. And one evening, I went there. And the first time for-- it was at a table, a tomato or a piece of bread, you know? And I was-- I was there. And there comes in an interesting story also, if I have the time? I have the time? Yeah, all right. During-- I mean, the time I went to-- one Jewish woman from the-- from the ghetto told that her mother gave away a fur coat, a fur coat, to a wife of a Polish doctor, Polish doctor. And since I didn't have no money, [INAUDIBLE]. So I decided to try to get from this woman this fur coat, you know, to sell it, or to be exchanged for food, you know? So this [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna, ?] who looked like a saint-- and was a saint. So she went to this wife, and asked-- [INAUDIBLE] remember the name, Dr. [? Rachiborski. ?] And the Germans took him away, her husband, the doctor-- probably killed him. And she told her about this fur coat. And after a few persuasions, she gave [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] this fur coat. And then we sold the-- the fur coat for a-- for flour, to make bread. For this helped to feed me [INAUDIBLE]. So I stayed at-- the-- at the name, [INAUDIBLE]. His name was Leonid. And one-- one was Zoya. And one was Liza. And we stayed there. They gave-- they-- it was their house. So they gave me one room, that I could stay by myself there, you know? Did you have any false name, false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. But they-- they were prepared to say, if somebody from the neighbors would see me and asked, they would say that I am a relative, that I came from another city, then-- a cousin or relative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you look like a relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I looked. Because I'll tell you why. I'll tell you why I looked [INAUDIBLE]. All right, so I was there. And then we were preparing '43 to Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1943 already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmastime. I-- they never-- religion-- they never asked me to change religion. They never-- this problem was nonexistent. And-- but I was already participating in the preparation for Christmas, you know? He got some eggs and butter from the Germans and whatever. And then we had one-- one brush for shaving. He had only one brush. And one day, he-- he comes to my room. And we shared the brush. And he said, I am going to town. Give me the brush. And I have to shave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave him the brush. And after five minutes, I hear the voice of Liza, one of this-- Grisha, Grisha, Grisha. I come in, into the room. And this young man is lying. He got a heart attack. And he was dead. And then the whole people from the neighborhood came in, you know. It's [NON-ENGLISH], a small town. And the doctor didn't come. And the doctor came in. Then I was scolding him. Why did it take him so long? I completely forgot at the time I was Jewish. I completely forgot that. I didn't-- you know, the only thing was [? Lonya. ?] Because I realized if [? Lonya ?] is not alive, I cannot be alive. [? Lonya ?] was this man who had died.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, [? Lonya, ?] yeah. And well, later, the doctor went. And the neighbors went. And-- and yeah, and I ran away from Lodz in one shirt. But it happened to be a shirt that [? Lonya ?] once expressed his opinion that he liked the shirt. And then Zoya came in and asked me whether I'll allow to use this shirt to prepare [? Lonya ?] for the funeral. And you can imagine that I didn't refuse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so they-- they dressed him into my shirt. And then they dressed him up. And then she comes in, and said to me, Grisha, you would like to see how [? Lonya ?] looks now? And I went to the room, where he was lying. And I was standing. And I didn't-- I didn't know what to say. I couldn't say a word. I was prepared to tell me, go, go [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't say anything. And then she said to me, the older sister, Grisha, you were here when [? Lonya ?] is alive. And you'll stay here now, when-- when [? Lonya ?] has gone. So I stayed there. And now comes in an interesting moment. What the Germans, usually they SS-- he was repairing for them the radios, you know? And they were coming in to pick up. And one day, she opens the door to my room. And two SS men-- you know, the SS men wore black leather long coats, you know? It was a special uniform. And then I see Zoya and these two SS men. That I did not give myself away was-- I cannot explain it. And she brought them in I should be the-- the-- to translate. Interpreter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I never knew German well. And I al-- and I decided never to say that I know German. Because you can give yourself away. Because German and-- and Yiddish-- you know, it-- so I-- they shook my hand. They introduced themselves. And I translated the-- the conversation. And they got their radio. And they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I say to Zoya, how could you do it? How could you not notify, not-- bring the two SS men into my room? How could you do it? And then she said, I don't understand. Why should they know that you are Jewish? You know, this-- this great feeling of-- of religion, or whatever it is, and this optimism-- I don't know how to explain it. But she said, how would they know? And they didn't-- they didn't know. They [INAUDIBLE]. And this was it, you know. Did she-- did she identify you as family member, as a friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she-- she didn't identify anything. She just brought them in. And-- and this was-- this was it. And then, well, I stayed there. And then the situation-- yeah. And then the mother of [? Lonya, ?] the one who died-- their mother lived-- she has divorced, of course. She was divorced. But their father lived in the same house that I lived, you see. But he was already a very strange-- you know, he was already senile, or [INAUDIBLE], you know. We stayed in here, in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he-- he was anti-Semitic. But this is a kind of anti-Semite that would never do anything wrong to you. You understand? He wouldn't denounce you. You understand? This is the type. How did you know he was anti-Semitic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he said it. He was open about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But he didn't-- he didn't do it. He didn't do anything. And I knew he wouldn't. He is not the type to do it, you know? And then his wife, divorced wife, who lived in a village, and was sewing, you know, and making a living. Her husband-- the second husband-- was a Polish officer. And the Russians took him away to Siberia-- probably killed him, among the other Polish officers that they killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came in and stayed in the same house with me, in the same house. And she was also anti-Semitic. But-- but she wouldn't go to denounce me. But the situation was a very interesting situation. There was a-- a place, like a big-- I don't know. What do you call it, for to put-- where you put in your clothing-- you know, closet or something. Closet.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I considered that in case they-- oh, I'm touching this, OK? I am touching this, OK? [? CREW: ?] OK. Then I always thought if somebody-- if the Germans come, or the police come, that I'll go behind this-- this closet. And she said to me, this won't help you. [LAUGHS] She-- and the situation-- the Germans started to-- to-- you know, to be defeated, under Stalingrad. You know, I started the story with Stalingrad. And every day, she was for the-- she was praying that the Germans are winning. Wanted-- she wanted the Germans to win. And of course, I wanted the other way. And every day she was clear-- you know, in a loud voice to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means, I hope that the Germans will beat the Russians, you know. And every day, there were better news.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh the-- the-- the-- I read newspapers. I read-- there are lots of Ukrainian newspapers. We got the news. And we had a radio. And we had-- we are the only ones that had a radio. You know, I saw the situation was such. But I still didn't-- couldn't hope that this will-- this will happen. And by-- and by the way, during the time, because we had the radio, we sometimes could catch London, London. And one evening, I caught London. And Yehuda [? Ben ?] [? Nuchim ?] was playing Kol Nidre in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you heard it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- you can imagine how I felt. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to describe. The language--

speaker: interviewer
text: Try.

speaker: interviewee
text: The language is too poor to describe feeling-- situations like this. So this-- the Germans were losing, losing. But it's a long way from Stalingrad to Krzemieniec. It's a long way, a long way. But the last-- the last few days, somebody came in and told these-- the sisters, you know, that the Germans are going to evacuate all men, all men, regardless of who it is, all men. And since I was a man, then what should we do? They'll come. And then, again, [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] knew somebody who lived-- among the mountains-- far away, the mountains. And they asked me to go there, you know. It was already getting dark. And on the way, a German officer stopped me, and asked me where is the [? Rosa ?] factory, where-- of tobacco somehow how are there. And he had to pick me up to ask me where this factory is. But I somehow-- so I managed not to give myself away. And I came to this place, to this guy. His name was [? Kluge. ?] And there were already about 40 men-- Ukrainian-- from town. Because they didn't want to be evacuated to a German labor camp [INAUDIBLE]. And they're expecting to. And that-- and taht night, we already heard the Russian-- you know, the Russian voices, and the shooting everything. And the Russians were-- were throwing some leaflets, leaflets in German, in German to demoralize the German soldiers, you know. And one of these-- they brought in one of this leaflet, you know. And I was so stupid to take this leaflet. And there are 40 Ukrainian men to translate it to them, this leaflet. This was the most stupid thing one can do. And-- but the reaction was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why was it stupid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stupid, because nobody of these Ukrainian knew the Germans would be able to translate a leaflet. The leaflets were in German. It was for the German soldiers. And in this population, this Ukrainian, you didn't expect-- nobody knew German. And if somebody knows German, then he must be Jewish. It's very easy to-- to conclude, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a-- but somehow, I saw one of them looked at me. And I-- this look was such that my impression was that he suspected something. And well, after a few hours, the Ger-- the Russians came in, came in. But the-- the-- the mood of the population, I knew, was very anti-Semitic. So [? Maria ?] [? Nikichna ?] was with me there. She was with me. And then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with the Ukrainians [INAUDIBLE]. And I ask her. I said, let's not tell anybody that you saved me. Let's not tell anybody. And this was-- and this was the end of my-- of my other-- how call it? The end of my suffering? And the end of my excite-- anxiety, and being-- and being present, being present to see all this holy curse in my own eyes, to be present then. This was bad enough. And I-- and the Holocaust still stays with me every night. When I go to bed, I-- I don't forget it.
